title: Freedmen's Bureau
summary: Established the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands in the War Department to provide relief, labor oversight, schooling support, legal assistance, and family reunification support for formerly enslaved people and war refugees.
outcome_summary: The Bureau became one of the federal government's most direct Reconstruction interventions, supporting schools, labor supervision, legal claims, and basic relief, but it was underfunded, politically contested, and largely withdrawn by the late 1860s.
impact_notes: Directly aimed at helping formerly enslaved people transition from slavery to freedom, even though its reach and durability were sharply limited by white resistance and federal retreat.

scores.notes: One of the clearest federal Reconstruction efforts on behalf of freedpeople, but its institutional life was short and its implementation uneven.

sources.notes: Official overview explaining the Bureau's creation on March 3, 1865 and its major functions.

sources.source_title: Records of the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands
sources.notes: Federal records guide describing Bureau operations, including schools, labor contracts, legal complaints, and claims.

title: Civil Rights Act of 1866
summary: Declared all persons born in the United States, except most Native Americans at the time, to be citizens and guaranteed equal rights to make contracts, sue, give evidence, and buy, lease, sell, and hold property.
outcome_summary: Passed over President Andrew Johnson's veto, it became Congress's first major civil-rights law and helped establish national citizenship and baseline civil equality for formerly enslaved people.
impact_notes: A foundational Reconstruction civil-rights statute that directly targeted the Black Codes and remains significant through later codification and constitutional development.

scores.notes: A cornerstone Reconstruction law with enduring legal significance, even though later courts narrowed some of its early promise.

sources.publisher: U.S. House of Representatives, History, Art & Archives
sources.notes: Official House history page describing the April 9, 1866 veto override and the law's core protections.

sources.source_title: Constitutional Amendments and Major Civil Rights Acts of Congress
sources.publisher: U.S. House of Representatives, History, Art & Archives
sources.notes: Official congressional historical data page summarizing the Act's main provisions and statute citation.

title: First Reconstruction Act
summary: Divided most former Confederate states into military districts and required new constitutions, ratification of the Fourteenth Amendment, and Black male suffrage as conditions for readmission to the Union.
outcome_summary: The Act reset federal Reconstruction on more expansive civil-rights terms, tying Southern readmission to constitutional change and Black political participation.
impact_notes: One of the central legal engines of Radical Reconstruction, though its protections depended on sustained federal enforcement that later collapsed.

scores.notes: Transformative in immediate effect, but structurally vulnerable to later political retreat and judicial narrowing.

sources.source_title: Constitutional Amendments and Major Civil Rights Acts of Congress

sources.notes: Official historical data page describing the First Reconstruction Act and its conditions for readmission.

sources.source_title: Power Struggle Over a New America
sources.publisher: U.S. House of Representatives, History, Art & Archives
sources.notes: Official House historical exhibit describing Andrew Johnson's resistance to Reconstruction legislation and congressional overrides.

title: Slaughter-House Cases
summary: The Supreme Court narrowly interpreted the Privileges or Immunities Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment, sharply limiting its usefulness as a federal protection against state-level rights violations.
outcome_summary: Although not a case brought by Black plaintiffs, the ruling weakened Reconstruction constitutional enforcement and narrowed one of the main pathways for protecting Black citizenship rights against hostile state action.
impact_notes: The decision did not abolish the Fourteenth Amendment, but it drastically reduced one of its most expansive clauses and contributed to later retrenchment in Black civil-rights protection.

scores.notes: A major doctrinal narrowing of Reconstruction constitutional power with long-lasting negative consequences.

sources.notes: Case text and decision date, including the Court's holding on the Privileges or Immunities Clause.

sources.source_title: Timeline of Events Leading to the Brown v. Board of Education Decision of 1954
sources.notes: National Archives educational timeline explaining how the Slaughter-House Cases weakened the power of the Fourteenth Amendment.

title: Civil Rights Act of 1875
summary: Barred discrimination in public accommodations and public conveyances and prohibited exclusion from jury service on the basis of race.
outcome_summary: It represented the high-water mark of Reconstruction public-accommodations policy, but key protections were later nullified by the Supreme Court in the Civil Rights Cases of 1883.
impact_notes: A major civil-rights advance on paper, but weakened at passage and later stripped of much of its force by hostile judicial interpretation.

scores.notes: Historically important and clearly pro-equality, but its practical durability was very poor.

sources.source_title: Constitutional Amendments and Major Civil Rights Acts of Congress
sources.publisher: U.S. House of Representatives, History, Art & Archives

title: United States v. Cruikshank
summary: The Supreme Court overturned federal convictions tied to the Colfax Massacre and sharply limited federal power to protect Black citizens from private racial violence and state-level rights violations.
outcome_summary: The ruling weakened Reconstruction enforcement by making it much harder for the federal government to prosecute racial terror and protect Black voting and civil rights when states failed or refused to act.
impact_notes: One of the most damaging Reconstruction decisions because it narrowed federal civil-rights enforcement after one of the era's deadliest massacres of Black citizens.

scores.notes: A major doctrinal retreat that emboldened white terror and undercut federal protection of Black citizenship rights.

sources.notes: Case text documenting the Court's narrowing of federal enforcement power.
National Archives overview connecting the Colfax Massacre to the Cruikshank ruling and its effect on Fourteenth Amendment enforcement.
